ontology.foodex2_description: Soup, meat-based — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for duck-based broth soup with vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for Kiam Chye Ark; A00GJ (meat-based soup) is the closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Duck with Salted Vegetable Soup is approximately 350 g (one bowl), providing about 385 kcal, 29.8 g protein, 25.2 g fat, and 8.75 g carbohydrate, with notably high sodium of approximately 1925 mg per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB entry for Kiam Chye Ark was located in the public HPB Food Composition Database. Nutrient values are adapted from the input record (data_source: hpb, provenance: canonical, confidence: 0.95), which cites HPB as the originating source. Values are consistent with duck-based broth soups with preserved vegetables in the HPB database. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central duck meat composition (USDA SR Legacy #05140) adjusted for soup dilution. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and moisture data for this traditional preparation are not individually reported in the HPB or USDA sources consulted.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kiam Chye Ark (Duck with Salted Vegetable Soup). GI estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat-based soups and broth dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The low GI is consistent with the dish's high protein content (8.5 g/100 g), moderate fat (7.2 g/100 g), and low carbohydrate content (2.5 g/100 g). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (35 × 8.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.1 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (550 mg/100 g; ~1925 mg per 350 g serving) from salted mustard greens (kiam chye) and preserved ingredients
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit consumption of Duck with Salted Vegetable Soup, as a single 350 g serving provides approximately 1925 mg sodium — exceeding 80% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and regular intake may significantly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (2.5 g/100 g) and high protein (8.5 g/100 g) with estimated low glycemic index (~35) support stable postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Duck with Salted Vegetable Soup is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content and high protein, which together produce a low glycemic load (~3.1 per serving); however, the very high sodium content warrants caution if the patient also has hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content and moderate fat content (7.2 g/100 g, including estimated 2.1 g/100 g saturated fat) from duck skin and meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Duck with Salted Vegetable Soup only occasionally and in small portions, as the high sodium load (~1925 mg per serving) poses a significant risk for fluid retention and elevated blood pressure, and the saturated fat from duck contributes to atherogenic lipid burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger in the recipe has documented anti-inflammatory properties; duck provides zinc and selenium which support immune function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Duck with Salted Vegetable Soup may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, as ginger contributes anti-inflammatory compounds and duck provides zinc and selenium; sodium intake should still be monitored if corticosteroids or NSAIDs affecting blood pressure are in use.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Duck with Salted Vegetable Soup, known in Teochew as 'Kiam Chye Ark' (咸菜鸭汤 / 鹹菜鴨湯), is a traditional slow-simmered broth dish central to Teochew and Peranakan culinary heritage in Singapore and southern China. The dish is prepared by slow-cooking duck — often leftover roast duck — together with kiam chye (salted mustard greens), ripe tomatoes, fresh ginger, and sour plum over several hours. The result is a deeply savory, tangy, and slightly sour broth with a complex umami character derived from the preserved vegetables and rendered duck fat. The duck meat becomes tender and absorbs the briny, acidic flavors of the salted greens and plum. Nutritionally, the soup provides approximately 110 kcal per 100 g, with 8.5 g protein, 7.2 g fat, and only 2.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35. The sodium content is very high at 550 mg/100 g (approximately 1925 mg per 350 g serving), primarily from the salted mustard greens, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant portion restriction. The dish is a staple family meal in Teochew households and is commonly served at lunch or dinner as a main dish or shared soup course. It is rich in B vitamins (niacin, B12, B6), zinc, selenium, and iron from the duck, and provides modest vitamin C and vitamin A from the tomatoes and vegetables. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic load, but sodium management is critical for those with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or diabetic nephropathy.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from the input record citing HPB Singapore as the originating database (provenance: canonical, confidence: 0.95), which is an approved primary source for Singaporean dishes. However, no exact HPB Foodo public entry for Kiam Chye Ark was independently verified during this processing run; values are treated as adapted from HPB. Saturated fat was estimated from USDA SR Legacy duck meat data adjusted for soup dilution. GI is an analogous estimate with no direct measurement available. Confidence is therefore rated medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for this traditional Teochew duck soup preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries consulted as of April 2026; these fields are therefore marked as data not available per the Veritas sentinel-value convention. saturated_fat_g (2.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #05140 (Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted) adjusted for soup dilution factor; this is an approximation. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat-based soups in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kiam Chye Ark exists in the published literature. The input record's dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based', which are factually incorrect for a duck-based dish and have been removed. The duplicate portion entry in the input has been deduplicated to a single 350 g serving. The input source 'Teochew International Federation' (teochew.org) is not an approved Veritas source and has been replaced with HPB Singapore as the primary nutrient source. Halal status is marked false as duck preparation with salted preserved ingredients may not conform to halal standards without specific certification; this should be verified.
